EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nor'easter moved up the east coast of the U.S. on the 19th, strengthening as it passed southeast of southern New England. This system was largely unremarkable with mostly sub-warning snowfall amounts in the 4-6 inch range. The exception was in far northeastern MA where 6-7 inches fell. Totals decreased toward Cape Cod canal and on the Cape only less than an inch was measured as precipitation fell as rain for most of the event. This event happened from late Sunday evening through before sunrise Monday morning causing some minimal slow downs but the fact that it was a holiday weekend helped reduce the impact.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 6-8 inches of snow fell with some of the highest amounts including 7.9 inches in Conway, 7.8 inches in Ashfield, and 7.2 inches in East Hawley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another clipper system moved across western Massachusetts during the evening hours on January 28 through the early morning hours on January 30, 2025. Moderate snowfall accumulations occurred across northern portions of Berkshire County where snowfall totals between 4 and 8 inches occurred. Lesser snow accumulations fell elsewhere. Strong winds developed once again on January 29 with widespread gusts between 40 and 55 mph with one instantaneous wind gust to 63 mph at the Pittsfield Airport. In addition, snow showers and localized snow squalls led to brief whiteout conditions, moderate to heavy snowfall rates and gusty winds on January 29.||Key impacts: travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall. Near zero visibility, over 35 mph winds, thunder and lightning and 1.1 inches of snow was observed near North Adams.

Western Massachusetts was impacted by an arctic airmass from January 21-22, 2025.The combination of below normal air temperatures, breezy winds, and nighttime radiational cooling led to minimum wind chill (or feels'-like temperatures) between -15 and -25 degrees Fahrenheit in the early morning hours on January 21 and January 22. The lowest of these air temperatures and wind chill values occurred at the highest elevations of the Berkshire Mountains.

An area of low pressure tracking from the Carolinas northeastward off the East Coast brought a widespread moderate to locally heavy snowfall to western Massachusetts on January 19-20, 2025. Snowfall amounts between 5 and 8 inches were common. Due to the conditions, the state Department of Transportation implemented a 40 mph speed limit on Interstate 90 across Berkshire County. Amid the heavy snowfall, the westbound travel lanes on Interstate 90 near Becket were closed due to a tractor trailer crash. No injuries were reported.||Key impacts: travel delays, road closures.

Cold air in place ahead of a low pressure system approaching from the Ohio Valley allowed snow to develop on the evening of February 15th. The main low pressure system passing to our west resulted in warmer mid level temps to overspread the region. This changed the snow to ice/rain by the early morning hours of February 16th.|A secondary coastal low helped keep shallow cold air in place across the interior and an extended period of icing. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level disturbance, together with a northward-tracking surface low from the Mid-Atlantic region brought light to moderate snowfall to western Massachusetts from late April 11 through early afternoon April 12. The heaviest snowfall was confined to the highest elevations of the Berkshire Mountains, though accumulations of one inch to 5 inches were common throughout Berkshire County. Much of this fell during the overnight period of April 11 and, as this occurred over a weekend, little impact to travel occurred, though slippery road conditions did occur. Additionally, patches of light freezing drizzle lingered into the evening hours on April 12 leading to icy roadways and reports of a few vehicles skidding off the road.||Key impacts: slippery travel conditions, minor traffic accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 3.6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dome of high pressure brought a period of very hot and humid conditions across western Massachusetts from June 23-24, 2025. Peak heat index values (or feels-like temperatures) were between 95 and 104 degrees Fahrenheit in many locations. Preliminary data from the U.S. Health and Human Services showed that eight-year records for one day heat-related emergency department visits were set during this period of hot weather across region 1 which included all of Massachusetts and the rest of New England.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Immediately on the heels of a complex winter storm that impacted western Massachusetts on February 15-16th, 2025, strong winds and bitter cold broke out across Berkshire County. Strongest wind gusts ranged from around 50 mph to 60 mph with a peak gust of 63 mph recorded at the North Adams Harriman-and-West Airport. Strong winds led to numerous reports of tree damage and subsequent road closures. The cold airmass inflicted across Berkshire County on the departing side of the storm of the days prior led to wind chill values anywhere from -1 to -12.||Key Impacts: power outages, tree damage, road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values ranged from around negative 10 to negative 12.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very hot and humid weather occurred in southern New England over the course of 4 days, peaking on Tuesday June 24th when heat index values reached 105 to 110. All four climate sites easily set daily records, and Providence and Boston set all time June high temperature records. There were reports of several dozen people treated for heat related illnesses, mostly at local beaches. Three recruits at the Massachusetts Firefighting Academy in Stow were hospitalized Wednesday morning after falling ill due to the extreme temperatures. Winds turning off the ocean finally broke the heat by June 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The nearest climate site in Boston (KBOS) reached 102F breaking the June high temperature record. Three prospective recruits at the Massachusetts Firefighting Academy in Stow were hospitalized on the morning of the 25th after falling ill due to the extreme temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex winter storm impacted western Massachusetts between February 15th and 16th, 2025 bringing snow, freezing rain, and strong winds. Snow accumulations ranged from nearly 5 to 8 inches across Berkshire County with flat ice accreting up to 0.25 to 0.30 inches. By the event's conclusion, the total liquid equivalent across Berkshire County ranged from about 1 to 1.25 inches. The combination of heavy wet snow, heavy ice, and gusty winds led to significant power outages across the region, in addition to numerous motor vehicle accidents, road closures, and subsequent travel delays. ||Key Impacts: power outages, motor vehicle accidents, travel delays, road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixed precipitation winter storm led to the combination of zone average snowfall of 7.7 inches, zone average flat ice accumulation of 0.27 inches, and zone average liquid equivalent of 1.25 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal storm brought widespread snow to interior southern New England with rain in the low elevations including the I-95 corridor. The storm dropped 6-8 inches of snow across the Berkshires and Worcester Hills with a dusting of snow in Boston on the back side. Impacts were limited.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 3 to 8 inches of snow fell with some of the higher totals including 8.9 inches in Ashburnham and 8.5 inches in Sterling.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds developed ahead of a potent frontal system in the highest terrain of the Berkshire Mountains on October 30th, 2025. The strongest gust was recorded at the North Adam���s Harriman and West Airport with an isolated magnitude of 44 mph. Elsewhere, wind gusts ranged from about 20-40 mph.

A very potent low pressure system brought widespread strong winds to much of western Massachusetts on Halloween (October 31), 2025. Widespread gusts of 40 to 50 mph were common, with isolated gusts nearing 60 mph, especially during the evening and late evening hours.

Sub-freezing temperatures ahead of an approaching warm front resulted in a period of accumulating freezing rain for all of western Massachusetts during the late afternoon and evening hours on December 28, 2025 into the overnight or morning hours on December 29, 2025. As temperatures rose above freezing, precipitation eventually changed to plain rain for many areas. Flat ice accumulations of a light glaze to 0.25 inches occurred which resulted in some slippery travel conditions and isolated power outages.

In the wake of the freezing rain/ice storm, a cold front crossed the region bringing a return to colder conditions as well as strong winds from the afternoon hours on December 29 through the afternoon hours on December 30, 2025. Wind gusts on the order of 40 to 55 mph were observed with a peak wind gust of 54 mph at the North Adams Harriman-and-West Airport.

In the wake of the freezing rain/ice storm, a cold front crossed the region bringing a return to colder conditions as well as strong winds from the afternoon hours on December 29 through the afternoon hours on December 30, 2025. Wind gusts on the order of 40 to 55 mph were observed with a peak wind gust of 54 mph at the North Adams Harriman-and-West Airport.

A complex, relatively progressive winter storm impacted western Massachusetts in the preliminary days of December, bringing widespread moderate to heavy snow particularly to portions of the Berkshire Mountains where terrain enhancement led to higher snowfall accumulations. ||An initial area of snow ahead of the main stratiform precipitation shield tracked through the region in the early morning hours of December 2, leading primarily to just a trace in northern Berkshire County. The main precipitation shield then moved in around or just after 7 AM, bringing moderate to heavy snowfall rates in areas where enhanced forcing created localized, embedded mesoscale snow bands. Snow concluded late December 2/early December 3 with total accumulations ranging from about 4 to 7 inches. ||Being that this was an early season event, acting like the first snowfall of the season, many traffic accidents were reported with slick road conditions throughout Berkshire County.||Key Impacts: Traffic accidents, travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 5.7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-freezing temperatures ahead of an approaching warm front resulted in a period of accumulating freezing rain for all of western Massachusetts during the late afternoon and evening hours on December 28, 2025 into the overnight or morning hours on December 29, 2025. As temperatures rose above freezing, precipitation eventually changed to plain rain for many areas. Flat ice accumulations of a light glaze to 0.25 inches occurred which resulted in some slippery travel conditions and isolated power outages.

Another complex winter storm impacted western Massachusetts, this time just after Christmas, December 26-27, 2025. After days worth of storm track shifting in the guidance, the resulting more northerly track of the storm brought widespread moderate to heavy snow across much of Berkshire County. The southern portion of the county saw the heaviest accumulations with about 6 to 7 inches commonly reported. The northern half, however, ended up on the lighter side of the storm with about 3 to 5 inches majorly reported. ||Key impacts: traffic accidents, travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 7.1 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system tracking from the Upper Great Lakes into Quebec brought a period of rainfall and strong winds to western Massachusetts from the evening hours on December 18 to the early morning hours on December 20, 2025. Winds started out southerly from the evening hours December 18 into the morning hours on December 19 before shifting to the west during the afternoon and evening hours on December 19 behind the passage of a strong cold front. Widespread wind gusts on the order of 40 to 55 mph were observed both ahead and behind the cold front with a peak wind gust of 56 mph measured at the Pittsfield Municipal Airport.

Windy conditions developed in the wake of a cold front on November 16, 2025. Peak wind gusts between 40 and 50 mph were observed across portions of western Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 46 mph wind gust was recorded at the Pittsfield Municipal Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a strong cold front, lake effect snow bands extended into western Massachusetts on November 28, 2025 bringing accumulations of 3 to 6 inches in some areas.||Key impacts: transportation delays.

Strong winds developed in the wake of a cold front during the afternoon hours on November 5 into the early morning hours on November 6, 2025. Wind gusts between 35 and 45 mph resulted in some localized downed trees and wires across Berkshire County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was reported down by an amateur radio on Blotz Road in the Town of Washington. A peak wind gust of 45 mph was recorded at both the Harriman-and-West Airport in North Adams and the Pittsfield Municipal Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports were received from amateur radio of trees and wires down on Blunt Road near South Egremont and a tree down near the intersection of McNerney Road and State Route 8 in the Town of Becket.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Southern New England in the afternoon bringing a round of severe storms which caused a wide swath of wind damage through MA with some in western CT. Some severe hail occurred in northern MA and a single storm dropped 5 tornadoes in central MA which led to widespread tree damage along but limited damage to structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Colrain, a tree and power lines were down on Van Nuys Rd.

Some severe hail occurred in northern MA and a single storm dropped 5 tornadoes in central MA which led to widespread tree damage along but limited damage to structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Southwick, a large tree and wires were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure brough some widely scattered heavy showers to RI and southeast MA which caused some localized flash flooding problems in MA and RI.
